key_insight: A stepped semi-coherent Cr-rich BCC/Ni-rich FCC eutectic interface enables strain-gradient-assisted dislocation activity, twinning, and interfacial shear so the ultrafine alloy can plastically co-deform at high strength without early cracking.
engineering_relevance: Interface design and nanoscale phase spacing can be used to push otherwise brittle high-strength alloys toward ductile behavior for additively manufactured structural components.
method: Electron-beam powder bed fusion to create an ultrafine Cr-Ni lamellar eutectic (~450 nm spacing)
Atomic-resolution STEM/HR-STEM to resolve interface structure, Kurdjumov-Sachs orientation relation, and local Ni enrichment
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension plus correlative TEM to track deformation mechanisms
evidence_signal: The abstract reports high flow stresses with large plastic strains and no cracking in situ, supported by correlative microscopy showing a sequence of dislocation accumulation, twinning, interfacial shear, and BCC dislocation activation.
watch_item: The mechanism is demonstrated on a specific additively manufactured Cr-Ni eutectic, so translating the behavior to other compositions or larger-scale parts may require careful control of interface spacing, chemistry, and processing defects.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
